title: Who Is Technology For? Ethics, Fairness, and Responsibility
description: Students evaluate technology ethics, audit stakeholders and harms, and apply responsible-design thinking to their own prototypes.
learningObjectives: Identify at least one ethical question raised by a technology decision.
Argue a position using evidence and reasoning.
Apply stakeholder and unintended-consequence analysis to product ideas.
Audit their own prototype and identify one ethically significant design decision.
prerequisites: Builds on Lesson 5 (AI bias), Lesson 6 (encryption, privacy), and Lesson 7 (design thinking, empathy).
materials: Sticky notes in two colors (agree and disagree)
fullDescription: Age group: Groep 5-8 (ages 8-12). Duration: 45-60 minutes. Devices required: none (fully unplugged). SLO alignment: Domain C (23A), Domain A (21D). Students practice ethics as structured reasoning, not right-answer recall, and apply it to real case studies and their own design work. Role model: Timnit Gebru, whose research exposed unequal AI performance and emphasized responsibility in technology development.

teacherInstructions.setup: Post vocabulary all lesson: Ethics, Stakeholder, Unintended Consequence, Transparency, Consent. Prepare agree/disagree sides for walking debate.
steps: Phase 1 (0-3 min): Spark question on whether technology can be useful and harmful at the same time.
Phase 2 (3-10 min): Walking debate with four provocation statements to surface value tensions.
Phase 3 (10-20 min): Concept intro with five ethical questions and examples for unintended consequences, stakeholders, and consent.
Phase 4 (20-42 min): Ethics audit challenge in two parts: case-study analysis then own-prototype audit from Lesson 7.
Phase 5 (42-47 min): Discussion on anonymization limits, design-change options, and builder responsibility.
Exercises (47-57 min): Complete responsible design checklist and unintended-consequences game; extension for fast finishers.
Share and reflect (52-57 min): Groups share one checklist question, class spots gaps, and students write personal ethical commitment sentence.
discussionPrompts: Does anonymized data become automatically ethical to sell? What evidence would you need?
If you discover harm in your own design, should you fix it, limit it, warn users, or stop building?
Is ethical responsibility part of the builder's job or only regulators and lawyers?
Can one product be fair for one group while unfair for another at the same time?
tips: Force clear positions in walking debate; no middle stance during response rounds.
Record tensions, do not rush to resolve them.
Encourage students to critique their own prototypes, not only the case study.
Treat discomfort during audit as productive evidence of deeper thinking.
Look for specific, actionable fixes rather than generic statements.

teacherGuide: Core message: ethical technology work means identifying who benefits, who is harmed, and who is excluded before launch. Students should move from abstract opinions to evidence-backed reasoning and specific design changes. The strongest outcome is when groups revise their own prototype after ethical critique.

studentExercises.title: Exercise 1 - Responsible Design Checklist
studentExercises.description: Create a five-question yes/no ethics checklist and apply it to your own prototype, writing one fix for each no answer.
instructions: Write exactly five yes/no ethical questions.
Include at least one question about people who are not the primary user.
Include at least one question about what happens when something goes wrong.
Apply all five questions to your own prototype.
For every no answer, write one concrete change that would make it yes.
successCriteria: Checklist questions are specific and testable.
Checklist covers consent, stakeholders, and risk or failure pathways.
At least one genuine no is identified with a concrete fix.

studentExercises.title: Exercise 2 - Unintended Consequences Game
studentExercises.description: For each technology, describe intended goal and one plausible unintended consequence affecting an overlooked group.
instructions: Use this sentence structure: 'The designers wanted [X], but an unintended consequence was [Y], which affected [Z].'
Technology 1: school canteen app from Lesson 7.
Technology 2: phone autocorrect system.
Technology 3: step-count fitness tracker.
Ensure each consequence is plausible and specific.
Share one strongest example with the class.
successCriteria: Each response includes intended goal, consequence, and affected stakeholders.
At least one consequence highlights an overlooked user group.
Examples are concrete rather than generic safety claims.

studentExercises.title: Extension - Write the Ethical Brief
studentExercises.description: Write a one-page ethical brief for one product option, including data use, stakeholders, launch questions, and harm scenario.
instructions: Choose one option: AI tutor for primary students, social platform for ages 8-12, or smart school bag with live location tracking.
State who product is for and who else it affects.
List what data is collected and who can see it.
Write three ethical questions the team must answer before launch.
Describe one realistic harm scenario that could happen even if product works as designed.
successCriteria: Brief covers users, non-users, data, and governance questions.
At least one harm scenario is specific and realistic.
Student shows balanced reasoning across benefits and risks.

studentChallenges.title: Walking Debate Sprint
studentChallenges.description: Take a clear ethical position on controversial statements, defend it, and respond to opposing reasons.
instructions: Move to agree or disagree side for each statement; no middle position.
State one evidence-based reason for your position.
Listen to one opposite-side reason and respond respectfully.
Track one statement where your own view changed after hearing others.
Write final position and reason on sticky note.
successCriteria: Student commits to a position and gives a reason.
Student engages with opposing view rather than repeating own claim.
At least one viewpoint shift or tension is documented.

studentChallenges.title: Ethics Audit of Your Prototype
studentChallenges.description: Apply the five ethics questions to your own Lesson 7 design and revise one feature based on risk or fairness findings.
instructions: Answer five audit questions: who helped, who harmed, who excluded, what could fail, who is responsible.
Identify one data, consent, or inclusion risk in your design.
Propose one specific design change to reduce the risk.
Attach ethics audit note to prototype.
Prepare to explain what changed and why.
successCriteria: Audit identifies at least one real ethical issue in own design.
Revision is specific and linked to audit finding.
Group can explain trade-off introduced by the change.
